text: I'm at <https://vis.social> but the nice thing about the fediverse is that picking the instance doesn't limit you (other than the benefit of the local timeline aligning with your interests)

attachments.title: vis.social
attachments.text: vis.social is an open social platform for creative people, especially anyone in sciArt, data, visualization, creative coding, and related arts and research. English is the common language of the instance.

text: I've tried applying to <https://merveilles.town> in the past, but my application was ignored / silently rejected... :sweat: <@UCUSW7WVD> has an account there, I wonder if they know any secrets required to get one...

attachments.title: Merveilles
attachments.text: Merveilles is a community project aimed at the establishment of new ways of speaking, seeing and organizing information — A culture that seeks augmentation through the arts of engineering and design. A warm welcome to any like-minded people who feel these ideals resonate with them.

text: Looks like Merveilles has changed to explicitly invite only, as I no longer see the "request account" path... I wonder if any existing users can invite people, or if it's admin only...

text:
I recently requested one from the admins. Hasn't actually gotten used yet, and it feels like a big enough deal that I'm not feeling comfortable asking immediately for another :sweat_smile:

<@UUQ2EQW21> how did you end up there? I actually got invited without asking, and by then I'd been following enough people from there that it felt worth uprooting myself.

<@UML4ZEKDK>, I think a link to a demo would go a long way here. Looking at <https://github.com/merveilles/The-Town#common-hashtags|https://github.com/merveilles/The-Town#common-hashtags> they don't seem to care about academic writing..

attachments.title: merveilles/The-Town
attachments.text: Resources for the Merveilles Town instance. Contribute to merveilles/The-Town development by creating an account on GitHub.

text: I got around to watching; thanks for the download version.  It is nice that you can test because you are still in text mode.  Those things are harder when you are trying to check graphics output.

elements.text: I got around to watching; thanks for the download version.  It is nice that you can test because you are still in text mode.  Those things are harder when you are trying to check graphics output.

text: Yes. I started thinking yesterday about providing some of the OpenGL interfaces without any graphics acceleration. If you have any ideas for ways to make OpenGL testable I'm all ears. Is it even something worth having, or are tests not a good model for graphics code?

elements.text: Yes. I started thinking yesterday about providing some of the OpenGL interfaces without any graphics acceleration. If you have any ideas for ways to make OpenGL testable I'm all ears. Is it even something worth having, or are tests not a good model for graphics code?

text:
I think the best way to test GL is to have a database of expected images and compare your output with that.  Then tolerate a small pixel % error.  Update your golden images when you change the spec, etc.
That's the only way I've seen it done.

elements.text:
I think the best way to test GL is to have a database of expected images and compare your output with that.  Then tolerate a small pixel % error.  Update your golden images when you change the spec, etc.
That's the only way I've seen it done.

text: It's tricky with GL, because different hardware draws things slightly differently too; maybe that's better these days, but it used to be quite a problem to validate the API when all the GPUs did slightly different rendering

elements.text: It's tricky with GL, because different hardware draws things slightly differently too; maybe that's better these days, but it used to be quite a problem to validate the API when all the GPUs did slightly different rendering

text:
Reminds me of <https://www.theguardian.com/music/2013/may/09/manu-chao-clandestino-culshaw|this article> about Manu Chao recording his classic album, Clandestino:
> '"Le hazard est mon ami" he likes to say – chance is his friend. They tried different different mixes on the children, aged three and six, who lived next door. "The ones they liked were the ones we chose," Manu recalled.'

text:
Fair! But still less open _in toto._ Also less repairable than my 9-year-old Thinkpad.

I've been trying (and failing) to live by a new ideal that some things are worth putting up with inconvenience for.

What we have today is not ideal. I'd love for RISC V to win. Or even ARM.
